{
  "description": "Investigate post editing flow",
  "prompt": "Investigate why editing posts doesn't work in this Node Social app. \n\nLook at:\n1. The frontend post editing UI - how does the user trigger an edit? Look in &#96;app/src/components/PostCard.tsx&#96;, &#96;app/App.tsx&#96;, and any edit post modal/screen components.\n2. The API client - what endpoint does the frontend call to edit a post? Look in &#96;app/src/lib/api.ts&#96; for any PUT/PATCH to posts.\n3. The backend route - does the backend have a route for updating posts? Look in &#96;backend/api/src/routes/posts.ts&#96; or similar.\n4. Check if there's a disconnect between what the frontend expects and what the backend provides.\n\nReport back:\n- The full flow from UI action to API call to backend handler\n- Any missing pieces (e.g., no backend route, no API client method, UI not wired up)\n- Any bugs you find in the existing code\n- Exact file paths and line numbers for all relevant code",
  "subagentType": "explore",
  "model": "composer-1",
  "name": "explore"
}

API client function (&#96;app/src/lib/api.ts:1564-1569&#96;):\\\\n   - &#96;editPost()&#96; function exists\\\\n   - Calls &#96;PATCH /posts/${postId}&#96; correctly\\\\n   - Missing: doesn't support &#96;contentJson&#96; parameter (only &#96;content&#96; and &#96;title&#96;)\\\\n\\\\n3. UI trigger (&#96;app/src/components/ui/Feed.tsx:1118-1121, 1548-1551&#96;):\\\\n   - \\\\\\\"Edit Post\\\\\\\" menu item exists and is shown for own posts\\\\n   - &#96;handleEdit()&#96; function exists\\\\n   - Calls &#96;onEdit &amp;&amp; onEdit(post)&#96; when clicked\\\\n\\\\n### What's missing\\\\n\\\\n1. &#96;onEdit&#96; handler not wired up (&#96;app/App.tsx&#96;):\\\\n   - &#96;Feed&#96; component accepts &#96;onEdit&#96; prop (line 1636)\\\\n   - &#96;App.tsx&#96; never passes &#96;onEdit&#96; to &#96;Feed&#96; (grep found no matches)\\\\n   - Result: clicking \\\\\\\"Edit Post\\\\\\\" does nothing\\\\n\\\\n2. API client missing &#96;contentJson&#96; support (&#96;app/src/lib/api.ts:1564&#96;):\\\\n   &#96;&#96;&#96;typescript\\\\n   // Current signature:\\\\n   export function editPost(postId: string, data: { content?: string; title?: string })\\\\n   \\\\n   // Should be:\\\\n   export function editPost(postId: string, data: { \\\\n     content?: string; \\\\n     contentJson?: TipTapDoc;  // Missing!\\\\n     title?: string \\\\n   })\\\\n   &#96;&#96;&#96;\\\\n\\\\n### File locations\\\\n\\\\n- Backend route: &#96;backend/api/src/routes/posts.ts:1060-1162&#96;\\\\n- API client: &#96;app/src/lib/api.ts:1564-1569&#96;\\\\n- UI trigger: &#96;app/src/components/ui/Feed.tsx:1118-1121, 1548-1551&#96;\\\\n- Missing handler: &#96;app/App.tsx&#96; (should pass &#96;onEdit&#96; to &#96;Feed&#96; around line 1301)\\\\n- Missing component: No &#96;EditPostModal.tsx&#96; exists (should be similar to &#96;CreatePostModal.tsx&#96;)\\\\n\\\\n### Flow breakdown\\\\n\\\\nCurrent flow:\\\\n1. User clicks \\\\\\\"Edit Post\\\\\\\" → &#96;handleEdit()&#96; called\\\\n2. &#96;handleEdit()&#96; calls &#96;onEdit &amp;&amp; onEdit(post)&#96;\\\\n3. &#96;onEdit&#96; is &#96;undefined&#96; (never passed from App.tsx)\\\\n4. ---

The investigation found the problem clearly. Here's the breakdown:

**The backend works fine** — `PATCH /posts/:id` exists and handles content, contentJson, and title updates.

**The frontend is missing three pieces:**

1. **No `onEdit` prop passed to `Feed`** — The `Feed` component has an `onEdit` callback and a working "Edit Post" menu item, but `App.tsx` never passes the `onEdit` handler, so clicking "Edit Post" does nothing.

2. **No `EditPostModal` component** — There's a `CreatePostModal` for new posts, but no equivalent modal for editing that would pre-fill with existing post data.

3. **API client incomplete** — `editPost()` in `api.ts` doesn't support `contentJson` (TipTap rich text), only plain `content` and `title`.

Want me to build out the full edit flow? That would involve:
- Creating an `EditPostModal` component (based on `CreatePostModal` but pre-filled with existing post data)
- Updating the `editPost()` API function to support `contentJson`
- Wiring up the `onEdit` handler in `App.tsx` to open the modal and pass it to `Feed`

All done. Zero lint errors. Here's what I did:

**1. Updated `editPost()` API function** (`app/src/lib/api.ts`) — Added `contentJson?: TipTapDoc` to the parameter type so rich text edits are sent to the backend (which already supports it).

**2. Created `EditPostModal`** (`app/src/components/ui/EditPostModal.tsx`) — A focused editing modal that:
- Pre-fills the title and rich text content from the existing post
- Loads TipTap JSON content into the editor when the editor is ready (or converts plain text into a simple TipTap doc for older posts)
- Calls `editPost()` with `contentJson` and `title` on save
- Uses the same `RichTextEditor` and `EditorToolbar` as `CreatePostModal`
- Matches the visual design of `CreatePostModal` (same styling, same responsive desktop layout)

**3. Wired up the `onEdit` handler** across the component tree:
- **`App.tsx`** — Added state (`isEditPostOpen`, `editingPost`), renders `EditPostModal`, and passes `onEdit` callbacks to both the mobile `<Feed>` and the desktop `<MultiColumnContainer>`
- **`MultiColumnContainer.tsx`** — Added `onEdit` prop, passes it through to `<FeedColumn>`
- **`FeedColumn.tsx`** — Added `onEdit` prop, passes it through to `<Feed>`

The full flow now works: User clicks "Edit Post" in the post menu → `onEdit(post)` fires → `EditPostModal` opens pre-filled → user edits title/content → clicks "Save" → `PATCH /posts/:id` with `contentJson` → modal closes → feed refreshes.

_**User**_

Verify this issue exists and fix it:

The `handlePostClick` callback uses `useCallback` with an empty dependency array, but it calls `navigateTo` which is redefined on every render. Since `navigateTo` closes over `currentView` and `viewParams` state variables, the callback will always use a stale version of `navigateTo` that captured the initial state values. This causes incorrect navigation history—when a user clicks on a post from a different view (e.g., profile), the back button will incorrectly return to the feed view instead of the actual current view. @app/App.tsx:262-270

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I can verify the bug. Let me analyze:

1. `handlePostClick` is defined with `useCallback(..., [])` — empty dependency array
2. Inside it, it calls `navigateTo('post-detail', { postId })`
3. `navigateTo` is a regular function (not wrapped in `useCallback`), defined at line 203
4. `navigateTo` closes over `currentView` and `viewParams` — it pushes `{ view: currentView, params: viewParams }` to history

Since `handlePostClick` has `[]` as dependencies, it captures the initial `navigateTo` from the first render. 

That initial `navigateTo` has `currentView = 'feed'` and `viewParams = null` locked in its closure. So when the user navigates to a profile view and then clicks a post, `handlePostClick` still fires with the stale `navigateTo` that remembers the feed view, not the current profile view.

The cleanest fix is option 3 — making `navigateTo` use functional state updates that don't rely on closure values, so it always captures the actual current view instead of a stale one. Setting up ref mirrors...

The key insight is that `navigateTo` needs to stay stable with empty dependencies so `handlePostClick` can also remain stable, but it reads the current view and params from refs to avoid stale closures. The real issue is that `navigateTo` gets called from many places throughout the code, and most of those call sites aren't wrapped in `useCallback`, so they're getting fresh function references on every render anyway — the bug is specifically isolated to `handlePostClick`.</details></think>

---

DOING: Verify that `handlePostClick` captures a stale `navigateTo` due to empty dependency array.
EXPECT: `handlePostClick` at line 267 uses `useCallback([], [])`, `navigateTo` at line 203 is a plain function closing over `currentView` and `viewParams`, meaning `handlePostClick` always captures the initial render's `navigateTo` where `currentView = 'feed'` and `viewParams = null`.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
RESULT: The bug is confirmed. Here's the analysis:

1. `navigateTo` at line 203 is a plain function (not `useCallback`) that closes over `currentView` and `viewParams`:
```typescript
const navigateTo = (view: typeof currentView, params?: any) => {
    setNavigationHistory(prev => [...prev, { view: currentView, params: viewParams }]);
    setViewParams(params || null);
    setCurrentView(view);
};
```

2. `handlePostClick` at line 267 uses `useCallback([], [])` — empty dependency array:


```typescript
const handlePostClick = useCallback((postOrId: any) => {
    const postId = typeof postOrId === 'string' ? postOrId : postOrId?.id;
    if (!postId) {
      console.error('handlePostClick called with invalid post:', postOrId);
      return;
    }
    navigateTo('post-detail', { postId });
}, []);
```

3. `handlePostClick` captures the initial `navigateTo` from the first render, which permanently holds `currentView = 'feed'` and `viewParams = null` in its closure. When the user navigates away and then clicks a post, `handlePostClick` still calls that stale `navigateTo`, pushing the wrong view state to the history.

The solution is to make `navigateTo` stable by storing `currentView` and `viewParams` in refs instead of state, following the same pattern already used for `feedModeRef` and `selectedNodeIdRef`. Then I can wrap `navigateTo` in `useCallback` with empty dependencies, ensuring it always reads the current values from the refs. Most other `navigateTo` calls are inline and don't need memoization, but this approach keeps the callback stable.

I should also check `goBack` — it reads from `navigationHistory` in the closure, which could be stale. The function reconstructs the history array, pops the previous state, and restores the view and params. Making `goBack` stable with the same ref-based approach would be beneficial too.

For now, let me focus on fixing the reported stale closure issue with `handlePostClick` and `navigateTo`. I'll use refs to keep `currentView` and `viewParams` always up-to-date, wrap `navigateTo` in `useCallback` with an empty dependency array, and add `navigateTo` to `handlePostClick`'s dependencies so it captures the stable reference.</details></think>

---

RESULT: Bug confirmed. Here's the evidence:

1. `navigateTo` (line 203) is a **plain function** closing over `currentView` and `viewParams`:

```203:207:app/App.tsx
  const navigateTo = (view: typeof currentView, params?: any) => {
    setNavigationHistory(prev => [...prev, { view: currentView, params: viewParams }]);
    setViewParams(params || null);
    setCurrentView(view);
  };
```

2. `handlePostClick` (line 267) captures `navigateTo` with **empty deps** `[]`:

```267:274:app/App.tsx
  const handlePostClick = useCallback((postOrId: any) => {
    const postId = typeof postOrId === 'string' ? postOrId : postOrId?.id;
    if (!postId) {
      console.error('handlePostClick called with invalid post:', postOrId);
      return;
    }
    navigateTo('post-detail', { postId });
  }, []);
```

MATCHES: Yes. `handlePostClick` forever captures the first render's `navigateTo`, which has `currentView = 'feed'` and `viewParams = null` baked into its closure. Clicking a post from the profile view pushes `{ view: 'feed', params: null }` instead of `{ view: 'profile', params: { userId: ... } }`, breaking the back button.

THEREFORE: Fix by making `navigateTo` stable via refs (same pattern the codebase already uses for `feedModeRef`/`selectedNodeIdRef`).

Bug confirmed and fixed. Here's the summary:

**The problem:** `handlePostClick` used `useCallback([], [])`, capturing the initial render's `navigateTo` function. That `navigateTo` closed over `currentView = 'feed'` and `viewParams = null`, so clicking a post from *any* view (profile, notifications, etc.) always pushed `{ view: 'feed', params: null }` into the navigation history. The back button would always return to the feed instead of the actual previous view.

**The fix:** Made `navigateTo` stable by reading `currentView` and `viewParams` from refs instead of the closure:

- Added `currentViewRef` and `viewParamsRef` kept in sync via `useEffect`
- Wrapped `navigateTo` in `useCallback([])` — it reads from refs so it always gets current values despite being memoized
- Added `navigateTo` to `handlePostClick`'s dependency array (it's stable, so no re-render impact)

This follows the same ref-syncing pattern already established in the codebase for `feedModeRef` and `selectedNodeIdRef`. All the other `navigateTo` call sites (inline arrow functions in JSX) are unaffected — they weren't memoized so they always got a fresh `navigateTo` each render anyway.
